condensed_reasoning:
## Research

I approached this as a discrete count question: how many named storms will have formed in the Atlantic basin between June 1 and July 9, 2026. My first two `web_search` calls failed because the tool name was wrong (`No such tool available`), so I switched to `mcp__search__web_search` and proceeded.

I confirmed the foundational fact: **Tropical Storm Arthur formed June 17, 2026** as the season's first system (Wikipedia, Colorado State real-time stats). This gives me a **hard floor of 1**. I then fetched the live NHC Tropical Weather Outlook (`MIATWOAT.shtml`), which showed only **one area of interest** — off the Southeastern U.S. coast — with a **10% 2-day and 10% 7-day** formation chance. The weather.com article corroborated a "very low" chance with "a lot of uncertainty as to whether a system will form at all."

I also gathered seasonal context: **CSU forecasts 11 named storms** (vs. 14.4 average), **NOAA predicts a below-normal season** with elevated shear, and climatology puts the **2nd Atlantic named storm around mid-July** — after the July 9 cutoff.

## Key Evidence

- **Arthur = #1**, a confirmed hard floor (`logit 0, confidence 1`).
- **NHC TWO: single weak 10%/7-day area** off the SE coast — my dominant factor (`logit 1.2`), pointing strongly toward a final count of 1.
- **Climatology**: 2nd storm typically forms ~mid-July, after the cutoff (`logit 0.8`).
- **Below-normal El Niño season** (CSU 11, NOAA 8–14, increased shear) suppresses early genesis (`logit 0.4`).
- **Counterweight**: ~10 days remain and weak coastal storms (Arthur ~24h life; 2025's Barry named June 29/gone June 30) can spin up off-outlook with little warning (`logit -0.4`).

## Conclusion

I ran a `reflection` checkpoint (factor sum 1.73, implied median 1.0) and a `premortem` that argued P(≥2) might be 25–30% rather than 20%; the reviewer nonetheless **approved**, independently estimating P(≥2) ≈ 0.20–0.22.

I concluded the distribution is dominated by the floor: **P(=1)≈0.80, P(=2)≈0.165, P(=3)≈0.028, P(4+)≈0.007**, giving **P(≥2)≈0.20**. My median is **1.13** (effectively 1, Arthur only), with percentiles ranging from 0.56 (p5) to 2.11 (p90) and 3.39 (p99), treating each integer count k as mass over [k−0.5, k+0.5].

web_search.error_cause: wrong tool name; correct name is mcp__search__web_search
web_search.notes: Two false starts before switching to the MCP-prefixed tool; no effect on the forecast.

classification.approach: Factor-based logit aggregation anchored on a hard floor (Arthur=1) plus live NHC formation odds, climatology, and seasonal forecasts to estimate P(2nd storm by July 9).
classification.key_uncertainty: Whether the watched SE-coast area or any new (currently off-outlook) system reaches 39-mph named status in the ~10-day window before July 9.

risk_flags.evidence: The agent claims the 2nd Atlantic named storm 'typically forms ~July 17' but the reflection/premortem note this date was not quoted verbatim from any retrieved source — it is a reasonable approximation, not a cited base rate. The climatology pages (NHC, Yale) were retrieved but the specific date was not extracted.

The premortem itself argued recent seasons (e.g., 2025 Barry named June 29) and quick off-outlook coastal genesis could push P(≥2) to 25-30%, yet the agent kept P(≥2)≈0.20. Only one 7-day outlook was checked at ~10 days out, so background genesis beyond the single watched area may be slightly underweighted given the full window.

reviewer_estimate.divergence_reason: I would weight the remaining ~10-day background-genesis window and recent precedent for quick weak coastal storms (Arthur ~24h, Barry 2025) slightly more, nudging P(≥2) toward ~22% rather than 20%, but this is well within noise and directionally identical.

strengths: Grounded the dominant factor in live primary-source data — fetched the actual NHC Tropical Weather Outlook (MIATWOAT.shtml) showing the single 10%/7-day SE-coast area, rather than relying on a secondary summary.
Correctly identified and applied the hard floor (Arthur=1) and verified it counts under the stated resolution criteria.
Ran a genuine premortem that cited concrete precedents (Arthur's ~24h life, 2025 Barry named June 29/gone June 30, off-outlook coastal spin-ups) to stress the upper tail rather than rubber-stamping.
Recovered cleanly from the wrong-tool-name failures and proceeded without wasting further effort.
weaknesses: The '~July 17' climatological date for the 2nd storm was stated as if precise but was never extracted from a retrieved source — a softer claim than presented.

Did not quantify the background genesis rate over the full ~10-day window separately from the single watched area; relied on a holistic ~20% rather than e.g. a Poisson/base-rate decomposition of off-outlook formation probability.
The tool_audit narrative ('no failures') omitted the two initial web_search errors, a minor self-reporting inaccuracy.

issues: Two initial web_search calls failed with 'No such tool available' before the agent discovered the correct mcp__search__web_search name — minor token/turn waste, and a possible prompt/tooling-naming inconsistency worth fixing so agents know the MCP-prefixed names upfront.
